does: This paper presents a comprehensive review and taxonomy of transformer-based language models, organizing them into six primary architectural families: encoder-only, decoder-only, encoder-decoder, long-context, permutation-based, and generator-discriminator variants. Beyond core architectures, it systematically examines post-2023 advances including instruction tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, direct preference optimization, mixture-of-experts scaling, and retrieval augmentation. The work then maps these technical capabilities across seven major application domains—healthcare, finance, legal, education, customer service, creative writing, and scientific research—identifying which architectural features and techniques make transformers suitable for specific use cases and establishing concrete connections between model capabilities and practical deployments.
connects: This review synthesizes decades of transformer research dating back to the original attention mechanism and the 2017 'Attention Is All You Need' architecture, building on subsequent foundational work on scaling laws, architectural variants, and pretraining objectives. It integrates insights from the rapid proliferation of model families including BERT, GPT, T5 and their successors, while incorporating recent methodological advances like RLHF that emerged from reinforcement learning and constitutional AI research. The work responds to the field's growing fragmentation and the difficulty practitioners face in distinguishing fundamental innovations from incremental variations, providing structure amid the pace of announcements from leading organizations like OpenAI, Google, Meta, Anthropic, Mistral and DeepSeek.
enables: This structured taxonomy and domain mapping enables practitioners to make informed architectural and methodological choices when developing applications rather than defaulting to the latest release. By linking specific transformer variants and training techniques to measurable capabilities across domains, the review provides a foundation for reasoning about model selection, fine-tuning strategies, and deployment decisions in specialized contexts. It sets up future work investigating domain-specific architectural optimizations, better understanding the interplay between scale, alignment techniques, and task performance, and developing frameworks for evaluating which innovations truly advance capabilities versus introducing complexity. The systematic coverage of current flagship models establishes a baseline for tracking which design patterns persist and which prove to be transient.
